item: Request full admin access and a list of all current members before touching anything
why: Making changes without full visibility can break workflows or lock out active users. Confirm you have owner-level permissions and a complete member roster before proceeding.

item: Export a full workspace backup via Settings → Export content as a safety snapshot
why: Irreversible deletions happen during cleanup. A full HTML or Markdown export gives you a restore reference point if something critical is accidentally removed.

item: Create a private 'Cleanup Staging' page to document findings, decisions, and items flagged for deletion
why: A running log prevents duplicate work, creates an audit trail for stakeholders, and helps you communicate what changed and why after the session ends.

item: Interview or survey 2–3 active team members to identify the 5 most-used pages or databases
why: You need a clear picture of what is actively relied upon before removing or restructuring anything. Protect high-traffic assets first.

item: Navigate to Settings → Members and remove deactivated, departed, or contractor accounts that no longer need access
why: Ghost accounts inflate member counts, expose sensitive data, and can retain edit permissions on pages long after someone has left the organization.

item: Audit every workspace-level Guest and review whether their page-level access is still appropriate
why: Guests are often added for a one-off project and forgotten. Each guest with broad access is a potential data exposure risk.

item: Review all pages set to 'Share to web' and disable public sharing on any that contain internal or sensitive information
why: Public pages are indexed by search engines and accessible without authentication. Many teams forget these are on after a public launch or demo.

item: Open the sidebar and identify all top-level pages that have not been edited in more than 90 days using the 'Last edited' metadata
why: Stale top-level pages are the primary source of workspace clutter. The 90-day threshold surfaces genuinely dormant content without catching seasonally-used pages.

item: Move all confirmed orphaned pages — pages with no parent, no backlinks, and no recent views — into a 'Pending Deletion' archive folder
why: Deleting immediately is risky. A 2-week archive window lets team members flag anything incorrectly identified before permanent removal.

item: Run a database audit: open each database, check for duplicate databases serving the same purpose, and merge or deprecate redundant ones
why: Teams frequently create new databases instead of expanding existing ones. Duplicate databases fragment data, create sync issues, and confuse new members.

item: Inspect every database schema and remove unused properties that have no data or have not been populated in over 60 days
why: Unused properties add visual noise, slow down database loading, and confuse contributors about what fields are actually required.

item: Check all Relation and Rollup properties across databases to confirm linked databases still exist and connections are intact
why: When a linked database is deleted or renamed, Relation fields break silently and Rollups return empty or incorrect values, corrupting downstream reporting.

item: Audit all active database views and delete saved views that are duplicates, have no filters, or belong to team members who have left
why: Orphaned views accumulate quickly and make the view-switcher unusable. Each database should have a clear default view plus intentionally named filtered views.

item: Define and document a naming convention (e.g., [TEAM] - [Type] - [Name]) and rename all top-level databases and pages to follow it
why: Inconsistent naming is the root cause of most search failures in Notion. A standardized format makes pages scannable and searchable within seconds.

item: Restructure the sidebar into a maximum of 5–7 top-level sections using clearly labeled teamspaces or parent pages
why: A flat, sprawling sidebar forces users to scroll endlessly. Grouping by team or workflow function reduces cognitive load and speeds up navigation.

item: Review all active Notion automations and integrations (Zapier, Make, API connections) and disable or update any pointing to deleted or renamed pages
why: Broken automations fail silently or error loudly and can orphan data. This step prevents downstream tool failures after restructuring.

item: Pin or favorite the 5 most-used databases and pages identified in your pre-work interviews to the sidebar for fast access
why: Quick wins in discoverability immediately demonstrate value from the cleanup and increase adoption of the restructured workspace.

item: Document the finalized structure, naming conventions, and permission rules in a 'Workspace Guide' page pinned at the top of the sidebar
why: Without documented standards, the workspace will return to chaos within months. A pinned guide sets expectations for new members and current contributors.

item: Share a summary of changes made, pages archived, and members removed with the team lead or stakeholders within 24 hours
why: Transparency builds trust in the cleanup process, allows stakeholders to flag missed items, and gives leadership visibility into the scope of the debt that existed.

item: Schedule a recurring quarterly 30-minute workspace hygiene review on the team calendar to prevent debt from accumulating again
why: A one-time cleanup without a maintenance cadence is temporary. Quarterly reviews catch stale pages, permission drift, and schema sprawl before they become unmanageable.
